They don't seem to recognize that, you know, there's a vast amount of territory behind the coastline of Iran, which can also be used to target. Uh, but either way, the coastline would be is a vast area. So, it doesn't really make much sense. Uh, especially if one hasn't planned for this in advance. But, um, well, what about Bondraas? That's a city of 580,000. Uh, they also have a uranium mine that's not far from the city where they harvest yellow cake. Uh they also have a desalinization plant there. The desalination plant could be destroyed. That would harm the people that live in Bondrabas. But I think they have some backup plans for that. But the bottom line is what are we going to do about that? Are the islands all sit in proximity to Bondrabas? If you can't control Bandrabas, what makes you think you can control these little islands? Other words, it's a it's an damned if you do and damned if you don't. And the Iranians may take the position, let them all land and then we just erase the islands. And see, this has been a problem that we've been dealing with in all of our war planning for years. You have the Marine Corps and the Navy that want to repeat World War II. They talk about island chains. Well, unless the island is about the size of Borneo, the island is going to be annihilated from the air. top attack, ballistic missiles, cruise missiles, drones. They'll all land on top of you and kill everybody on top of the island. And we've attached so much significance to little islands without understanding they no longer hold the strategic value that they did 80 plus years ago. They're just an invitation to lose people. And that's the other issue. Assuming you get on there and you can survive for any length of time, how do you feed yourself? How do you evacuate wounded? And then ultimately, how do you get out when it becomes clear this is a dumb idea? At least the British and the Dardinels were able to pull vast numbers of ships into the dinels and systematically evacuate the 500,000 troops that were there. That's not so easy in the Persian Gulf. You, you know, you're if you stand above Car Island and look down, you're on top of an escarment. All of the terrain is very high and it's very, very steep. So what are you going to do on those islands? The other thing is the notion that all of the oil will cease to move at that point is false. The Iranians have other means of exporting oil. Now they're making a lot of money right now. That's obvious. They're charging toll. But again, you you listen to the Chinese, the Russians, the Indians, and others. They're very intelligently saying, "Look, we need to end this. We'll get together. We'll have a commission. Maybe we sign a treaty that that guarantees access to the Straight of Hormuz. He did that after World War I for the dinels. We have a treaty. The Turks control the strait, but it's all carefully measured. It's all carefully managed. The Turks do not arbitrarily say you can come and you can't. They haven't done business that way. And that can be organized as well. But the key thing is we can't be in the Gulf. Our time there is now over. And if we can't be in the Gulf, I don't know where else we're going to be. Well, this could have fit within Trump's wider plan. If the goal was really to pull out of Europe and the Middle East, then uh well, this is really the time uh he can clearly argue that the Europeans weren't reliable, that the Middle East should look after their own problem and then focus on the Western Hemisphere in East Asia if this is the main objective. Uh but a key problem though is uh Israel here because the US and Israel do appear to be joined by the hip and um Israel of course has been pushing hard for this war for several decades now it finally has the war. If Trump now decides uh you know no matter how he wants to end it even if he just declares victory we don't need the straight of moose we'll go home. Uh where does that leave Israel? because if they're locked in a war with Iran, um, which they don't want to end, uh, is the US still able to leave? And if the US is able to leave, what do you do with a vulnerable Israel armed with nuclear weapons, which uh, is not ready to give up this fight against Iran? I mean it's uh you know one doesn't want to be hyperbolic but on the other hand this is a very strange situation to be in and there's a lot of desperation and a lot of countries have bet all or nothing on this conflict. Oh Glenn you're absolutely right. The key thing to remember about NATO is that once you leave the beltway you drive across America the vast majority of people don't even know what it is. And if you if people if you ask people, do you know that we have US Army ground troops in Germany? They'll say, "Really? I didn't know we had any troops in Germany." And some people who are who are moderately smart and educated will say, "Why after 80 years do we still have troops in Germany?" So I I don't think that's a big deal here at home. Now you'll have all sorts of people in Washington who will hurl abuse at at Donald Trump for destroying NATO, the greatest alliance. It's all crap. Nobody cares. The electorate does not care about that. But they care about the economy. And this is accelerating very bad trends. If you talk to someone like Alistister Mloud, he says this is the end of fiat currency. And all you have to do is point to the rise in the value of gold. And as gold goes up, the value of the dollar goes down, down, down. We are seeing in real time the yuan pro take shape. In other words, instead of a petro dollar, you get the pro yuan. I think that's coming. And the interesting part is it's going to be backed by gold. China has been hoarding gold, purchasing gold, buying gold. It has a vault in Riad, a vault in Hong Kong. It's real. H that's the future of bricks. that's where they're headed. Now, will it will it ultimately encompass a basket of precious metals or something? I have no idea. But it's got to be based on some tangible concrete value. And right now, that's gold. Now, we can't do that. We just can't. Uh so, what do we do? Do we watch our currency go down the go down the toilet? A lot of people think that's where we're headed. Nasim Talib, you know him, the man who originated this idea of the black swan. He's been saying for months, and I agree with him, that frankly speaking, gold is now the reserve currency. It's not the dollar. And if yuan is backed by gold, well, I guess yuan will become the prowan. So, I think we've lost we've actually lost by this stupid action all because of Israel's greater Israel imagination. and and aspirations. And because the Israelis hate all of their neighbors, think all of their neighbors are inferior and deserve to be essentially exploited, bullied, and and run and and governed by them, it's not going to happen. So, how does this end then? I mean, if Trump doesn't pull back, again, it's hard to say. he made some reference to this but uh again he pretended to do peace negotiations uh uh you know during two surprise attacks. So again, I I I do think at some level he he doesn't want this war that his intentions from not just the first presidency, but the second was to avoid these kind of things, but now that it's in this war and he can't really uh well afford to pull out, it seems. Uh what are the options for Washington here? Can they I mean do they put boots on the ground? Do they do they try to invade Yemen? because you know if Yemen shuts down the Red Sea I know it's a huge country it's like what 34 million people it's I I wouldn't want to enter this country but uh again desperate situation results in foolish policy so what do you expect to happen from here on well one of two things either he announces I've decided unilaterally to end our involvement in this war and I welcome the opportunity to negotiate with the Iranians and to assist in any way possible to return the Persian Gulf to normaly. Uh that's an admission that he made a mistake which is fine. He made a mistake. Uh I don't know if that'll happen or not. Uh personally I would welcome that development but I think the Israel connection really makes it impossible at this stage. Uh we don't understand the connection that Israel has to Trump. I don't understand all of it, but I understand enough to know that yes, you're right. When I spoke to him in uh April of 2020 in the Oval Office, it was supposed to be short turned into a longer session. And I was enormously impressed with the things that he had to say. And one of the things that was very clear to me is he didn't want a war with Iran. In fact, he was looking for some sort of negotiated agreement. And I'm I'm sure he felt that way when he was elected. However, uh these Zionist billionaires were very instrumental in his winning that election. Not only because of the money he was given, his campaign was given. Not only because of the many ways in which his family and he have been enriched by the Zionists, it's also by what they didn't do. They didn't provide any support whatsoever to his opponent. And the last time around when he lost the election or lost bid for re-election, they invested heavily in his opponent. So he knew they could deliver the goods. In other words, if he had them on his side, he could win. But when he did that, he had to make some promises. And I think one of those was that he would back Israel's attack on Iran. Uh whether or not he was aware that Israel's plan is to turn most of the region into the faximile of Gaza, I don't know. But that's really Israel's answer for Iran, Gaza. And wherewise we started out with this decapitation nonsense. And I think a lot of that goes back to Israel. We can get the leadership. If we get the leadership, this will internal encourage internal upheaval and so forth. So then they lied of about 30,000 people being killed. That wasn't true. It was about 3,000. and they had a very important role in stirring up that violence, passing out weapons and encouraging people to shoot at police and so forth. It failed. So then the decision was we'll try the decapitation. That'll probably work. It didn't work. So what is the goal now? Well, since lawyers of London shut down the straight of Hormuz and the Iranians can now charge people to move through it, uh the idea is that you have to destroy Iran. period. The destruction of Iran, the disintegration of its society. And after all, that's what we wanted to do to Russia. That's what the Europeans don't understand. That's what we wanted to do. And when I say we, I'm not talking about the average American. I'm talking about the same people that are backing Trump. Now, we're very interested in the same thing in Russia. Destroy Russia, rape it, divide it, fragment it, steal its resources. Well, now we're turning to Iran with the same purpose in mind. And I just don't know that Donald Trump can get off this train to hell. And that's what he's doing. He's riding a train to hell. But just as a last question, how do you see Russia responding to this? because uh I always make the point the Russians were genuinely very optimistic when Trump got reelected because uh with the Russia gate thing being over and Trump seemingly having the genuine objective of improving bilateral relations with Russia be it either for you know common views on the world in terms of you know conservatism or just pragmatic great power politics that is getting Russia on our side of the ledger instead are pushing it towards Iran. It's nonetheless uh whatever the reason was it seemed genuine and now of course one year into this it's you know the US much like the Europeans are still participating in the proxy war and the surprise attack on Iran appears to have shook the Russians a bit that is the willingness to throw out the rule book kill leaders you know attack nuclear power plants uh across the board they they also lost some faith in the whole diplomacy how do you see Russia responding to this because on one hand I see the disappointment but there's many who also seem to have a hope that uh you know there won't be another Trump someone who really actually wants peace with Russia if he does want peace of course as you said it's a even though it's Trump behind the wheel nonetheless it's the same objective nonetheless it seems to destroy to destroy Russia break it up and seize its resources Well, first of all, the Russians do not understand how the American government works. Very few people beyond the borders of the United States do. The most astute observation that Putin has made is that he's spoken with several presidents and then discovered that nothing really changes because others are in control. Now, some people call it the deep state. Uh, okay. I call it Zionist billionaires, people that are among the wealthiest in the world. They dominate Wall Street. They are financial capitalists. These are not people who made money from building anything, from creating anything. These are the kings of uh financial transaction. And they have enormous quantities of money and they're willing to employ that money in pursuit of their agenda. We talked about what the agenda in Russia originally was. Now we're looking at the same agenda. The good news for them is that if this works, then Israel will be in charge of the entire region in their minds. And that means that they from their positions in New York and London and the banks, they're controlling everything because ultimately that's what they want to do. They want to control the region's resources. Now, my view is that's not going to happen. And I think that the Russians are now figuring out it probably won't happen. If anything, we have demonstrated not our brilliance and our supremacy in every field. We've demonstrated that our superiority isn't that great. That our effectiveness is not what they thought it was. That there are more chinks in our armor than either the Russians or the Chinese or anybody else really really thought. That's the danger of using military power when you don't need to. There was no pressing reason for us to attack Iran. None. And when you have to go back to Netanyahu talking points into 1982 and you know the bombing of a building that contained Marines where the Marine Guards had not even been issued ammunition. What a disaster. That's your justification for destroying a nation of 93 million people. Or that they simply say things you don't like, but they're a threat to Israel. Okay. I think the biggest threat to Israel other than Israel itself at this stage is probably Turkey. And in fact that's what Naftali Bennett has said and even Netanyahu has admitted that which means I guess the expectation is as long as they control us and our armed forces that if we're successful at utterly destroying Iran then we can turn our attention to do the same thing to Turkey. Disaster. Insane. So NATO is finished. NATO died on the battlefields of Ukraine. It's over. The Russians need to wake up and and discover that they not anyone else, but they are going to have to settle this war. The only way I think they can do that is by advancing. So, we'll see in two weeks when the ground is thoroughly dried out whether or not President Putin is willing to finally end the war. And he can do that. He has the military power to do it. There's nothing really standing in his way. He can obliterate all the opposition, but he's got to do it. No one is going to help him. No one is going to offer him a good deal. Doesn't exist. And Trump, no matter what he privately thinks, has demonstrated he can't deliver. Now, we're dealing with the Gulf, and the Russians are watching this. And I think the Russians have always advised the Iranians to be conservative, to cooperate, to negotiate. Whether or not they continue to do that at this stage, I don't know. But I think that now more than ever, the Russians and the Chinese will back Iran. So I see no evidence that whatever we do is going to result in the outcome that the Israelis want. Well, uh, sorry, let me squeeze in one very brief last question. Wait a minute now. You said that was the last, Glenn. This better be a short one. I got to go. Well, uh, I know you hate this word when, uh, well, what is the strategy here of the Europeans, you think? because uh well they they seem to have bet everything on backing the US hoping to win over the love of the Americans but but now suddenly they have this you know they they Iran is essentially the conflict where they decide to turn the back on the US well what do you think is the strategy now because they don't have energy from Russia from the the Middle East their American backup is kind of gone Ukraine is falling apart the Middle East again the mess how How do they make sense of their current positions? Keep in mind that in 1964-65, Prime Minister Harold McMillan was approached and asked whether or not the British would support the the Americans if they intervened in Vietnam and he said very in a very straightforward manner, "Absolutely not. We have no intention of involving ourselves there." Did that destroy our relationship with Britain? No, of course not. But in those times, people understood the limitations of treaties and alliances and the alliance with us pertained primarily to the Soviet threat. So I think I I think it's you're still going to have uh bilateral relations between us and the Europeans regardless of what happens. They buy a lot from us. We sell them a lot. We buy a lot from them. That's still unchanging. But the energy question is critical and they are not prepared to go on the same path to extinction that the Gulf Emirates are on. The Gulf Emirates are finished. I don't see those being rebuilt at all. We are not coming back. That was very clear from Trump when he said, "Well, we'll just leave and that's it. We've done as much as we can or we'll do whatever we can to obliterate Iran, after which we will leave, too." All right. So the Gulf Emirates have bet on the wrong horse and they have cultivated hostility for their big neighbor. They're paying a terrible price for it. The the Germans, the Poles, the French, the British have done the same stupid thing towards Russia. They have cultivated an enemy in the big neighbor to the east. Huge mistake. Unnecessary. You know that. I know that. Russia has no interest in invading or threatening Western Europe. That means you need new governments. You got to get rid of of mounts for God's sakes. I was so happy to see the AFD announced quite recently, we st we support the complete removal of all US forces from Germany. Absolutely should have happened 30 years ago. We should never have stayed in that country. There's no justification for it whatsoever. And I think others are going to reach that conclusion. De Gaul was right. We should remember that. Orbond has been right. The British need a new government badly and so do the French. And before they get that or when they get it, they may end up fighting an internal rebellion of various types and sorts as David Betts has pointed out. I don't know if you've had David Betts on your program or not. I did. But he is absolutely right. So these are things that have to be dealt with that have to be addressed and can only be addressed by the Europeans. said, "While they do this, put an end to the hostility with Russia and tell these maniacs, I like them, they're wonderful people, Lithuanians, Latvians, Estonians, and the Baltic states, stop. Stop the stupidity. You've got to live with your neighbor and get along with your neighbor. It's not that scary an idea." And by the way, that's what Bismar told them back in 1879. Knock it off. Get along with the Russians. whatever you think you're suffering from, it's not as bad as you think it is. And he was right. It wasn't. It got a lot worse under the Soviets. That's a different era, but that was catastrophic. We We got to get out of this business of treating Russia as this perpetual Stalinist threat. They're not. Well, thank you for accommodating my second last question and as always, uh, thank you for your time. You owe me, Glenn. You owe me. I'll make it up to you someday. Thank you. See you. Bye-bye.

videos.title: Guerra do Irã AO VIVO | “RISCO DE GUERRA NUCLEAR É REAL” | Trump intensifica ataque | Coronel Mac...
videos.description:
#Irã #Trump #Israel

Barkha Dutt entra AO VIVO com o renomado analista militar Douglas Macgregor enquanto Donald Trump se dirige à nação, prometendo intensificar os ataques dos EUA contra o Irã nas próximas 2 a 3 semanas para "terminar o trabalho". O anúncio fez com que os preços globais do petróleo disparassem, decepcionando os mercados que esperavam uma redução da escalada na guerra com o Irã.

Macgregor classifica o conflito como "insano" e afirma que o discurso de Trump ficou muito aquém das expectativas. Ele argumenta que o envolvimento dos EUA está intimamente ligado aos objetivos estratégicos de Israel, alertando que o risco de uma guerra regional mais ampla está crescendo, incluindo o perigo real de uma escalada nuclear.

Em uma forte mensagem para a Índia, Macgregor insta Narendra Modi a intervir diplomaticamente e dialogar com Trump, alertando que as consequências econômicas, especialmente o aumento dos preços dos combustíveis, podem em breve afetar as famílias indianas.

A conversa também examina a possibilidade de uma operação terrestre limitada dos EUA no Irã, destacando os sérios riscos que ela representa para as tropas americanas, a estabilidade do Oriente Médio e a economia global.
videos.transcript: Pesquisar transcrição A very very good morning to all of our viewers in India and across the world. It's a little short of 7:30 in the morning here in India and of course uh getting to be about 10:00 at night in the United States of America where the American President Donald Trump has moments ago finished his address to the nation. Now, for those who are anticipating that he would either announce the exit of the United States from a war that has created cataclysmic and catastrophic consequences around the world, well, that exit announcement, the end of the war did not happen. for those who were looking to Trump's address to the nation to indicate whether the next step would be some sort of ground invasion, limited ground operation, commando action. Well, clearly the American president didn't spell that out and perhaps he couldn't have been expected to either. But what he did indicate is that American troops would be moving ahead to quote unquote finish the job. He said that this was because this was the only way to honor American troops who had been placed in harm's way in this war. It is not the news that the markets, the global economy, and much of the world would have wanted to hear. He did set a time frame, however, the same time frame that we've been hearing for his from his administration for the last many days, two to three weeks. He said most goals, most strategic objectives of the United States of America had been achieved and very shortly and he put a number on it two to three weeks the task would be completely finished. He said talks the negotiations that he had earlier claimed were going really well would continue but if there was no deal then Iran was going to be hit very very hard. The bottom line we simply do not know where we stand. All we know is that there is no end yet to this war. In a moment from now, we'll be going live to the United States of America to talk to a top global military analyst, a geopolitical risk analyst, somebody who's also been a former adviser to the US Secretary of Defense. We will be joined by Colonel Douglas McGregor in a couple of minutes. I'm Barcadat, you're with the Mojo story. On the program today on this special live broadcast, we are going to be decoding Trump's address to the nation. As I said, for those who were expecting that he was going to announce the exit or the end of the war, that did not happen. Instead, Trump doubled down and spoke about how it's going to take at least another two to three weeks before America can achieve all of its objectives. He claimed that most of these objectives had nearly been achieved and in two to three weeks they would be absolutely finished. He used the phrase finish the job to describe what the troops were planning to do next. Ahead of the American president's address to the nation, the A10 attack fleet, the Thunderbolt, had already begun movement towards West Asia, raising anticipation and apprehensions of what Trump's planning next. Would it be some sort of ground invasion? Would it be a limited commando action? We simply do not know. In the meanwhile, it continued to be a night of uh war. Uh for its part, Iran has continued to target uh Israel. Tel Aviv came under intense and enormous bombardment. this even as Iran's president, president Khan released a letter directly addressing uh the American people to say that the American people were not the enemy of Iran. We should remind our audience that Trump's approval ratings are at an all-time low, historically low for any American president and this war is extremely unpopular for him at home. And yet that address to the nation did not bring any closure to the war. I want to introduce our newsmaker early this morning night for him in the United States of America. Colonel Douglas McGregor is with us. He's a top global military analyst. He's a geopolitical risk adviser. He's been uh an adviser formerly to the US Secretary of Defense. We appreciate your time uh Colonel. Uh it's good to see you and I just want to get straight to it because I know you're a busy man and we've got limited time with you. disappointed with the president's speech. Yes, I would say disappointment is an understatement. I had really hoped that he would take into account the really terrible impact this war is having on the global economy. You know, again, our friends around the world are being harmed. The Japanese, the Koreans, you, India is being harmed. Uh you do $180 billion dollars in trade every year with the Gulf States. Those Gulf states, I don't think, are going to survive now. They're in bad shape at this point, but I think the next two to three weeks could be devastating. And it's not just the Gulf States. I I think the whole global economy is in trouble. And I was hoping that he would change course. I really was because he's using a sledgehammer to fix a cell phone. This is a 20th century method of operation. You could have seen this happen in Korea, in Vietnam, and we did. Uh, and and I can tell you frankly, these these massive bombing attacks, even though they become more price, more precise over time, they're devastating, but they're not effective. And you know, if this has been so successful, why is it that every time he gives a speech and talks about how great everything is, you know, the Iranians launch tactical and theater ballistic missiles at all of their opponents and drones. I mean there's no evidence that the capability to reach out with this connectivity, thisworked ISR, intelligence, surveillance, reconnaissance in space and in the terrestrial environment that's also with all the strike systems all over Iran. Some people call it a mosaic strategy, but they're distributed. They're all over the country. They can converge and deliver enormous precise firepower very quickly. I don't see any evidence that that capability has been seriously affected. And they have these underground missile cities and there are there's a lot underground that's stored that hasn't been used. So I I'm I'm discouraged to hear him say those things. The other part of this is if we're so successful, what do we need ground troops for? I thought that this brilliant air naval complex was going to win without any ground troops. They weren't necessary. Of course, the initial expectation was that Iran would fall apart. 96 hours. And now we're talking about bombing Iran into the Stone Age. That's insane. Why? What interest could we possibly have in doing that to the Iranian people? And he says that's where they belong. I mean, these are ridiculous statements that no president, certainly none in my lifetime that I can remember, would have ever made ever about anybody. So, I I think it's the wrong approach. I don't think it's going to work. And I think that the use of ground forces is very dangerous because the same precision weapons, the same uh Chinese and Russian provided satellite coverage is going to be very dangerous for them. It's it's going to it's going to put anybody we put on the ground over there uh in a very difficult and dangerous position. So, I don't see how this opens the straight of Hormuse. I I just don't uh I I'll get to the straight of hormones in just a moment, but may I ask you, Colonel, uh listening to his speech and reading between the lines, is ground action or attempted ground action by American troops now inevitable? Because what else are they going to be doing for the next two or three weeks? You know, I I can't say. Uh they obviously have built up a substantial number of them. I think it's going to reach 20,000 very shortly. Uh but the question is how do you get them there? Most of the United States Navy is hundreds of miles away from the Persian Gulf. They discovered that if they were close, they were going to be struck by missiles. They would be at risk. And so, the Navy has moved away. Now, the Houthis have joined the fight. I suspect the Houthis will probably close the entrance to the Red Sea, which means now you're you're dealing with enemies on on two flanks. You're having to fight in multiple directions at the same time. We're We probably have restocked our air defense missiles, our air and missile defense missiles, but I don't know how many and I don't know how many we still have. We we've obviously used thousands of them. I say that because every time an incoming missile is directed at us, we usually fire two missiles back. Uh so I I I don't know how long we can keep up an intensive bombardment. Now, I think we we expended something like 11,000 precisiong guided missiles and munitions in the first several days at the cost of billions and billions of dollars and that didn't work. I suspect we're going to do that again. But at the same time, you pointed out in just when you came in, you talked about the A10. That is a ground support weapon. That's exclusively what it's used for. And it's very good, but it too is vulnerable. And uh I heard the president say that the Iranians have no air defense. He said they have no anti-aircraft ability. That's not true. Uh and that's not true particularly when you're dealing with ground troops because you've got to come in lower to support them effectively. So at any rate, uh I'm I'm very very disappointed and I'm afraid things are going to get a lot worse. Can we talk about the uh the ATM the Thunderbolt fleet for just a moment? uh this fleet was actually moved even before Trump addressed the nation and it led to widespread speculation in American media and across the world as you mentioned that this is a ground support weapon. What is your biggest fear right now that he's going to go in for some crazed uh what he thinks is a limited ground operation either to take the Cara island or Fox News was reporting uh that he has taken a briefing on whether it's possible to have a commando operation to move uranium. It all sounds frankly insane. And I use that word because it's a word you've used for this war. When you looked at the A10 fleet being moved, when you hear about all this news about the sort of ground ops he's considering as a top military man, what do you think? Do you go, "Oh my god, what's going through your head right now?" Well, you're putting uh small numbers of troops. And you know, I know people will say, "Well, 10,000, that's a big number." But you're not going to put 10,000 on one of these islands. So you're putting small numbers of people in a very vulnerable position. If you're light infantry, the most powerful weapon you have in your organization, frankly, is the radio because it allows you to reach aircraft and artillery. Now, I don't think we're going to get much closein artillery support from the fleet because the fleet's afraid of being sunk, which means that you're entirely dependent upon air power. Now, the A10 is lethal because it can orbit above you at what what looks like a leisurely pace. It has excellent optics. It can pick up lots of information and identify targets on the ground. And its cannon 30mm and 105 millimeter cannon are very, very accurate. If they can sit up there and orbit without being shot down, they can have a terrible impact on the enemy and they can help keep the the infantry on the ground alive, but they may not be able to. During the Kosovo air campaign, which I was involved with, we used some A-10s. And the A-10 found that whenever it dropped below 15,000 ft, all the aircraft discovered this, they were immediately taken under very effective fire by the Serbs. Well, you also have to look at this very compartmentalized terrain. You mean the Iranian coast is like a wall of mountains and you can sit on the other side of the plateau and up on those ridgeel lines and you can command everything on the Gulf, which means that you can take aircraft under fire from those locations as well. Unfortunately, I think we've killed most of them at this point because a lot of our bombings in and around Tyrron have struck neighborhoods where you had the country's intellectual leadership and in many cases what I would call middle and upper middle class Iranians who want a different kind of government, a more secular government. But I think the whole region is now turning decisively against Israel. Now, I would exclude the uh uh Gulf elites, but those people have sold out to everybody. They were not unreasonable because their focus was to get to the peace the negotiating table and to stop the killing, stop the war. very similar to what Trump has tried to do, he says he's tried to do with Ukraine. And I think it's unfortunate that it has not it has not precipitated anything. As far as I know, there's been no formal response. I also know that anything that we sent to the Pakistanis that were that was supposed to be transferred to Iran has elicited the same response from Iran over and over and over again. We're not negotiating and I don't think they will because the strategic initiative is in their hands. Yeah. Yeah. Do you see a role for India? Would you would you India has kept quiet and focused on getting our own people our ships out. Do look at Go ahead sir. Just wanted to point out that Hexith in the meanwhile has a tweet out on X called back to the stone age. I just don't understand this way of talking but anyway. Yeah, it's it's horrendous. Uh yeah, I I think uh India should get off the sidelines. Now you have what 9.2 9.3 million of your citizens that have been working in the Persian Gulf region. I don't know if they've been evacuated or not. I'm sure some have left and I'm sure and I'm sure Prime Minister Modi is very concerned about that which is appropriate. He's obviously or or his foreign minister has talked directly. Your foreign minister is exceptionally gifted and brilliant man. By the way, I'm sure he's talked to the Iranians and it's quite clear to me that the Iranians want good relations with you and are going to work to get the tankers through the straight of war moves. Uh, so I think that's all good, but I think uh, Prime Minister Modi should make the effort to contact President Trump directly because Prime Minister Modi has good relations with Israel. If the if he is is in a position to influence things, the Israelis are going to be much more comfortable with whatever agreement he comes up with or he helps to negotiate than they will be with Islamabad. And again, I'm not impinging Islamabad. I'm not saying they are not sincere. What I'm saying is that Prime Minister Modi is much better positioned to do that. Now, I don't know what's actually going on. I don't know if your foreign minister has spoken with Secretary Rubio or with President Trump. I hope he has because India really is a great power. You have a lot at stake in in this war and this war is is coming to your kitchens. It's going to affect your farmers. it's going to affect your your lifestyle, your standard of living. So, I don't think this is the time to wish everybody well and stand on the sidelines. I think roll up your sleeves and get involved with this because if you don't, this this train has left the station and it's the train to hell and we don't all need to ride it. Absolutely. I I know that Trump and PM Modi have spoken uh but I think India has stayed out publicly and I think what I'm hearing you say Colonel is that it's time to actually get involved to bring the principles to some sort of agreement here. Just a last question. I know you've got a heart out in a minute. You know, I spent about 11 days in the Gulf uh trying to get the perspective from there and I was able to go right up to the edge of the straight of Hormuz from the Gulf of Oman. And I met a mariner and he told me and he told me this 5 days ago. He said, "If this war doesn't end in a week, it's going to push the global economy back by at least four to five years. What is your biggest fear right now? And if you could advise Trump, if you met Trump, let's say you just walked into Donald Trump and you had to say one thing to him for the sake of America, for the sake of the world, what would that be? We're going to end the conflict right here and now. We want to sit down and talk with the leadership of Iran and hammer out some mutually acceptable agreement. And if they don't want to speak to us, that's where Prime Minister Modi comes in. Then they can talk to others. But we're stopping. Now, the problem for President Trump, though, is Israel. Israel wants the destruction of Iran and the destruction of the rest of the world. it seems in the process. If it if it serves their purposes, I suspect that's true. Well, Colonel Douglas McGregor, I've got to thank you for never pulling your punches. Uh I know you're so widely followed here in India. I've got like people are just in the audience stream saying thank you for getting him. He was absolutely the best voice to hear right after that extremely disappointing address to the nation by Donald Trump. We thank you for your time. We're going to let you go because you're much in demand, but we hope to reconnect with you again very soon. Uh, I would have hoped in better times, but it looks like this war isn't ending anytime soon. Thank you very much, sir. Take care. Thank you as well. My best to everybody. Thank you. Bye-bye. And we leave our audience now with the key points of Donald Trump's speech where he said, "Finish the job and put a two to three week timeline on this war ending." How he came up with this figure only he knows. Take a look. Sir, please finish the job. Every one of them. and we are going to finish the job and we're going to finish it very fast. We're getting very close. I want to thank our allies shortly. We are going to hit them extremely hard over the next two to three weeks. We're going to bring them back to the stone ages where they belong. It's great to see you here. Thank you for watching our work. videos.title: "Catástrofe total": Coronel Macgregor critica duramente Trump em reação impulsiva ao discurso sob...
videos.description:
O Coronel Douglas Macgregor (aposentado) é um consultor de risco geopolítico com décadas de experiência em defesa e política externa dos EUA.

Nesta edição do ANI Broadcast com Ishaan Prakash, o Coronel Macgregor apresenta sua primeira reação ao discurso de Trump sobre o Irã, chamando-o de "teatro do absurdo" e alertando para uma catástrofe iminente. Ele afirma que Trump "perdeu o rumo" e que a guerra vai piorar.

#ANIBroadcast #IshaanPrakash #Macgregor #DonaldTrump #Irã #Hormuz #PetróleoBruto #Netanyahu #Israel #Golfo #IdadeDaPedra

Marcadores de tempo

00:00 – Discurso de Trump sobre a guerra com o Irã
05:23 – Ameaça de atingir o Irã “com extrema força”
06:41 – Usinas de dessalinização do Irã
08:03 – Riscos para os países do Golfo
10:41 – Aumento da presença militar dos EUA no Golfo
14:50 – Riscos das operações terrestres no Irã
18:13 – Negociações de paz EUA-Irã
20:30 – Trump e a OTAN
22:29 – O papel do Paquistão nas negociações de paz

videos.title: Douglas Macgregor: A Guerra do Irã Destruiu a OTAN, os Estados do Golfo, Israel e o Império Ameri...
videos.description:
Douglas Macgregor é um coronel aposentado, veterano de combate e ex-conselheiro sênior do Secretário de Defesa dos EUA. O Coronel Macgregor argumenta que a Guerra do Irã contribuiu para a destruição da OTAN, dos Estados do Golfo, de Israel e do Império Americano. (Gravado em 1º de abril de 2026)

Yeah, good point. But well, we heard here that the that the US already won the war. At the same time, he also argues that the US is ready to leave and the straight of Hermoose should be the problem of the Europeans and the regional states. Well I'm not sure a decision has been made uh or if it has he's not sharing it. I think it's a very strange set of circumstances. I mean, you have the claim, this incessant drum beat, we've already won. We destroyed everything that could be destroyed. Uh, they're down to nothing. They can't keep up. They're running out of missiles. Uh, their back is against the wall, etc., etc., etc. Well, if that's true, why is it that every time he makes some sort of statement along these lines, a wave of missile attacks comes out of Iran? uh tactical theater ballistic missiles, drones that do enormous damage to Israel and to frankly the Gulf Emirates. I mean, if that's really the case, why hasn't Iran already hoisted the the white flag? I mean, I I just don't I think that those claims have to be dismissed up front. What I am hearing from people who are on the inside that I trust is that we have perhaps reduced uh Iran's uh ballistic missile capability from 100% down to perhaps 70%. Uh that their air defense is being very effective against the unmanned surveillance aircraft selectively against other kinds of aircraft but has not performed as well as they'd hoped. But they have always been willing to absorb the strikes. They've always known that their infrastructure would be brutalized and they think the the payoff is is worth the trouble. So that's the first thing. Second thing is if things are going so well and we've had so much success with the air and missile campaign, why do we want to introduce ground troops into the equation? Doesn't seem to make a lot of sense. So I don't think you're getting a truthful assessment from the US side. I think the president is desperate. I think he faces public humiliation inside the United States as well as overseas if he admits the truth. And when he starts talking about, well, maybe I'll just leave and dump all of this into the laps of Europeans, we ought to stop and and consider a few things. First of all, commercial traffic through the straight of Hormuz has fallen by about 95 to 97%. uh 15 million barrels of oil a day have been taken offline. The physical shortages have already hit so hard that right now Singapore jet fuel is selling at 231 dollars a barrel. And as long as the war persists, the real people who shut down the straight of Hormuz, who are not Iranians, they're sitting in London. They're called Lloyds of London. The insurance brokers aren't going to ensure anything that goes through the straight right now because it's a war zone. So if you really want to open up the straight, you've got to stop the war. Uh simply passing an ultimatum against to the Iranians and then turning to the Europeans who were really never consulted on this whole business. Neither were the Japanese. Neither were the South Koreans. I mean, frankly, we ignored all of our allies and simply acted independently in a way that we decided to act and it was never really in our interest to do so. When you turn to a friendly country country like India, you discover the Indians are apppoplelectic. Well, they do $180 billion of business and trade a year with the Emirates. Suddenly that's gone. And if we continue to destroy infrastructure in Iran, the the Iranians have said, "Fine, if you hit our deselination plants, if you hit our oil infrastructure, we're going to turn around and obliterate everything in the Persian Gulf." So what does he plan to do? He wants to land troops on some islands presumably. Right now the United States Navy sails 500 to 800 miles away from the Gulf and stays on the move cutting giant squares in the ocean because it doesn't want to be sunk. It doesn't want its ships on the bottom. Uh how do you get the troops in there? Can you get close enough without being detected and without coming on fire to sort of put the troops in a position from which they can attack something? Then how do you propose to attack wherever you want to attack? Can you account for all the potential air defense systems that might be there, including shoulder fired missiles? uh can you ensure that you've neutralized this brilliant network of intelligence surveillance reconnaissance platforms with uh strike systems that are all over Iran and they're miles deep inside the country anywhere from 10 miles to 200 miles to 300 miles. You know, all of these questions are never really answered. It's the big handwave. Oh, we're successful. We're brilliant. We've done everything right. Uh, these pesky Iranians are too dumb to surrender, but we think we've done enough damage. Now, from whom have we not heard? Who is really in charge? Israel. Why are we in this war? Because we suddenly decided that Iran was this satanic evil element in the international system that had to be purged for anyone anywhere in civilization to be safe. No, that's a lot of nonsense. We're there because Israel wants Iran taken off the board, which is the the phrase used by the neocons in Washington. This whole thing was sold from the very beginning on the on the assumption that first of all, this will be a short campaign. Uh we would be surprised if they didn't fall apart in 96 hours. That's what I was told. Secondly, uh once we've taken Iran off the board, Israel is safe. It has no worries. It can bully uh the region. It can push its power and influence to the limits. And oh, don't worry about the Turks. We'll keep them under control. Uh we're close with the Turks. Turks don't want trouble with us. They'll do what we say. So, the attitude in Israel was fine. And I think tonight he he recognizes he's got to go one way or the other. He's got to say, "It's over. We've won. We're leaving." In which case, Israel is in trouble because Israel hasn't won. and Israel must have us backing them unconditionally to win anything. So I don't think the Israelis will approve of that. And it's very hard for me to believe that the immensely powerful scionist billionaires in the United States who are real really the foundation for the Israel lobby's influence and power are prepared to throw Israel under the bus as they say. So, I I I find that hard to imagine. And perhaps President Trump will say, "Well, we're going to continue to support Israel." Well, that's like saying, uh, you know, I have all these dead cats, but I'm going to continue feeding them as long as possible because that's what Israel looks like right now. It's in ruins. It's not getting any better. We don't know how many people have died, but certainly a lot more than the Israelis were prepared to accept. I think that's pretty obvious. Uh, so what do the Iranians want? Well, the Iranians want us out of the Persian Gulf. What have we done for decades? The Sykes Pico states that were created at Versailles in the aftermath of World War I, gone. We don't accept that anymore. So, what do they want besides us out of the Gulf? They're not going to come to peace terms with the Israelis as long as the Israelis are expanding into Lebanon and killing people there by the thousands or expanding into Syria. So, how do we deliver on Israel? Are we really in control? I don't think we are. Yeah. No, I spoke to a to an Iranian as well who who made a point that if they go up the escalation ladder, they can do a lot of different things. Uh some of most of them I would have predicted, but one of them I thought was interesting that is uh to reward Iraq for its assistance. They could also change the borders of the region since you you know mentioned the Sykes Pico that is uh the Kuwait could go be joining Iraq essentially. So there there's a lot of um surprises which seems to be outside our control at the moment in terms of how the region will be impacted by this. Uh what I wanted to ask as well though was how do you see the region beyond um uh the Middle East being affected? That is uh especially the NATO relationship. I find this to be interesting that is uh for on one hand Trump has been arguing that NATO is now a paper tiger. Marco Rubio said perhaps we should just uh you know we have to reconsider this relationship. They're only there when we need to help them not the other way around. And I kind of can understand from both sides that is from the European perspective the Americans never told them uh didn't inform them never cared and then now they want them to go on the suicide mission into the straight of moose. On the other side the Europeans blocked the access to military bases airspace. Uh Poland was asked to hand over some patriot batteries to help out the United States in the region and they declined. So, I kind of can understand where the mutual frustration comes from. But what what do you think this war though will do to the transatlantic partnership? Uh several years ago in 1991 during the air campaign uh while most of us that were prepared to invade Iraq were sitting on the sidelines watching the air campaign unfold in front of us. We were literally literally up on the Saudi border watching all of the sorties every night that went in with bombers and various kinds of aircraft. And one night uh this was very early in the morning actually. This would have been about 4 in the morning. Uh I was trying to get some rest and I was suddenly awakened by a soldier. He said, "Sir, quickly cut get to the CP. We need you right now." I said, "Okay, what's happening?" He said, "Well, we have a report that we're under fire." I said, "Really?" Thought that was interesting. I didn't think the Iraqis during the air campaign would risk an attack, but all things are possible. So I got up, walked into the uh command post and I said, 'Now what's going on? Said, 'Well, ghost troop, that was G troop has an observation post up here. That meant a couple of Bradley's uh up on the border and they say they're under fire. I said, what? You know, get this man on the phone. Well, this man on the phone was very uh we we have airto ground bombs uh falling on our location. Well, I've never heard of anything other than airto ground bombs. I haven't heard of any anti-gravity bombs. And I said, "What the hell is this man sober?" Of course, there was no liquor to consume. So, I assumed he was. And the next thing I get is, "Well, uh, we we need help. We need help." And one of the sergeants who was there was a very smart non-commissioned officer. He said, "Yeah, that's a great idea. Let's all get under the artillery fire with ghost troop." In [laughter] other words, if everybody up there is under fire, why are we going up to join them? Why don't we do something intelligent and try to find out where it's coming from and whatever. Anybody who thinks that American soldiers are stupid is wrong. They're actually very intelligent people. So, finally, I got the executive officer on the phone and he said, "Look, I'm sorry about this. This man had been watching flares that were dropped by A10s and others. And these flares were dropped to distract air defense weaponry so that the missiles ended up being diverted by the flares and instead of striking the target. I think that's kind of what Donald Trump is asking the Europeans to do. Hey, we did something immensely stupid. We're sending the United States into a recession and most of the world is at risk of going into a depression. All because we went into the Persian Gulf for Israel and we've set back, you know, the world for god knows how long, 10 years before we recover because food, fuel, feed stocks, fertilizer, the big ones right there, the four Fs are screwing us. You know, we're screwed. Thank you so much. Yeah, let's all go down to the Persian Gulf and contribute to this catastrophe. And Trump said, well, if you're not going to help us, we don't need you. You know, this goes back to the false assumption that the North Atlantic Treaty was designed for anything other than defense and the defense was against the Soviet Union. Period. That's it. And ever since the Soviet state system collapsed, we've been trying to repurpose and repurpose the famous statement, if NATO is not out of area, well then it's out of business. We don't need it. And I told everybody in NATO, get smart. Get out. You don't want to go to all of the hell holes that we have identified for invasion and occupation. But Europeans, well, you know, we're afraid and uh we really need the Americans here. It's vital to our security. Okay, fine. Here we sit. Now the Europeans are saying, this is a dumb idea. Don't really want to participate in this. And why aren't we talking to the Iranians? Because the Iranians have said, "Look, the straight of Hormuz is closed to countries unfriendly to Iran. That's all." Which means even though the numbers of tankers used to average 75 a day going through the straight, now it's 3 to four. But that number can rise provided the tankers and their destinations are not to places unfriendly to Iran. So many people are saying, "Well, let's talk to the Iranians." And the Americans are saying that is unacceptable. You can't talk to those people. If you do that, you're going back to Munich. You're negotiating with Hitler. Yes. Here you go again. So, we're in a bit of a pickle. Uh, and I think the Iranians hold the strategic initiative. They are not at the end of their rope. I think he's got to make a decision. Do I bail on this? It's a stinker. I made a mistake. find a way to get out and blame it on other people, which is the standard Trump approach, or do I say, "No, I'm going to obliterate you. You know, you refuse to negotiate with me, the great marketer, the great salesman, then I'm going to wage a warning air and missile attack, and I'm going to bring in ground troops, and I'm going to cut you off for the Gulf and force the Gulf open." And I think the Iranians are sitting there saying, "Okay, go ahead." Because if you're going to put ground troops anywhere in that area, you have to neutralize all the strike weapons, neutralize the intelligence, surveillance, reconnaissance network, stop all potential air defense, including shoulder fired missiles. Because if you can't do that, first of all, they may never reach the destination. They may be shot out of the sky before they get there because they're going to have to fly in. I don't see a massive amphibious assault working. That means that they have to get there without already being identified in Kuwait or Bahrain or the UAE. And uh don't see that happening. So I think the Iranians hold the strategic initiative and we stand an excellent chance of ending up like a a giant 14 or 15 or 16 or 18 wheel truck headed down the side of a mountain for a concrete wall. we'll just end up battering ourselves against a concrete wall one more time. I've heard of many commentators argue that uh you know if the US could just control the coastline then they would uh be able to control the straight of her moose. They don't seem to recognize that, you know, there's a vast amount of territory behind the coastline of Iran, which can also be used to target. Uh, but either way, the coastline would be is a vast area. So, it doesn't really make much sense. Uh, especially if one hasn't planned for this in advance. But, um, well, what about Bondraas? That's a city of 580,000. Uh, they also have a uranium mine that's not far from the city where they harvest yellow cake. Uh they also have a desalinization plant there. The desalination plant could be destroyed. That would harm the people that live in Bondrabas. But I think they have some backup plans for that. But the bottom line is what are we going to do about that? Are the islands all sit in proximity to Bondrabas? If you can't control Bandrabas, what makes you think you can control these little islands? Other words, it's a it's an damned if you do and damned if you don't. And the Iranians may take the position, let them all land and then we just erase the islands. And see, this has been a problem that we've been dealing with in all of our war planning for years. You have the Marine Corps and the Navy that want to repeat World War II. They talk about island chains. Well, unless the island is about the size of Borneo, the island is going to be annihilated from the air. top attack, ballistic missiles, cruise missiles, drones. They'll all land on top of you and kill everybody on top of the island. And we've attached so much significance to little islands without understanding they no longer hold the strategic value that they did 80 plus years ago. They're just an invitation to lose people. And that's the other issue. Assuming you get on there and you can survive for any length of time, how do you feed yourself? How do you evacuate wounded? And then ultimately, how do you get out when it becomes clear this is a dumb idea? At least the British and the Dardinels were able to pull vast numbers of ships into the dinels and systematically evacuate the 500,000 troops that were there. That's not so easy in the Persian Gulf. You, you know, you're if you stand above Car Island and look down, you're on top of an escarment. All of the terrain is very high and it's very, very steep. So what are you going to do on those islands? The other thing is the notion that all of the oil will cease to move at that point is false. The Iranians have other means of exporting oil. Now they're making a lot of money right now. That's obvious. They're charging toll. But again, you you listen to the Chinese, the Russians, the Indians, and others. They're very intelligently saying, "Look, we need to end this. We'll get together. We'll have a commission. Maybe we sign a treaty that that guarantees access to the Straight of Hormuz. He did that after World War I for the dinels. We have a treaty. The Turks control the strait, but it's all carefully measured. It's all carefully managed. The Turks do not arbitrarily say you can come and you can't. They haven't done business that way. And that can be organized as well. But the key thing is we can't be in the Gulf. Our time there is now over. And if we can't be in the Gulf, I don't know where else we're going to be. Well, this could have fit within Trump's wider plan. If the goal was really to pull out of Europe and the Middle East, then uh well, this is really the time uh he can clearly argue that the Europeans weren't reliable, that the Middle East should look after their own problem and then focus on the Western Hemisphere in East Asia if this is the main objective. Uh but a key problem though is uh Israel here because the US and Israel do appear to be joined by the hip and um Israel of course has been pushing hard for this war for several decades now it finally has the war. If Trump now decides uh you know no matter how he wants to end it even if he just declares victory we don't need the straight of moose we'll go home. Uh where does that leave Israel? because if they're locked in a war with Iran, um, which they don't want to end, uh, is the US still able to leave? And if the US is able to leave, what do you do with a vulnerable Israel armed with nuclear weapons, which uh, is not ready to give up this fight against Iran? I mean it's uh you know one doesn't want to be hyperbolic but on the other hand this is a very strange situation to be in and there's a lot of desperation and a lot of countries have bet all or nothing on this conflict. Oh Glenn you're absolutely right. The key thing to remember about NATO is that once you leave the beltway you drive across America the vast majority of people don't even know what it is. And if you if people if you ask people, do you know that we have US Army ground troops in Germany? They'll say, "Really? I didn't know we had any troops in Germany." And some people who are who are moderately smart and educated will say, "Why after 80 years do we still have troops in Germany?" So I I don't think that's a big deal here at home. Now you'll have all sorts of people in Washington who will hurl abuse at at Donald Trump for destroying NATO, the greatest alliance. It's all crap. Nobody cares. The electorate does not care about that. But they care about the economy. And this is accelerating very bad trends. If you talk to someone like Alistister Mloud, he says this is the end of fiat currency. And all you have to do is point to the rise in the value of gold. And as gold goes up, the value of the dollar goes down, down, down. We are seeing in real time the yuan pro take shape. In other words, instead of a petro dollar, you get the pro yuan. I think that's coming. And the interesting part is it's going to be backed by gold. China has been hoarding gold, purchasing gold, buying gold. It has a vault in Riad, a vault in Hong Kong. It's real. H that's the future of bricks. that's where they're headed. Now, will it will it ultimately encompass a basket of precious metals or something? I have no idea. But it's got to be based on some tangible concrete value. And right now, that's gold. Now, we can't do that. We just can't. Uh so, what do we do? Do we watch our currency go down the go down the toilet? A lot of people think that's where we're headed. Nasim Talib, you know him, the man who originated this idea of the black swan. He's been saying for months, and I agree with him, that frankly speaking, gold is now the reserve currency. It's not the dollar. And if yuan is backed by gold, well, I guess yuan will become the prowan. So, I think we've lost we've actually lost by this stupid action all because of Israel's greater Israel imagination. and and aspirations. And because the Israelis hate all of their neighbors, think all of their neighbors are inferior and deserve to be essentially exploited, bullied, and and run and and governed by them, it's not going to happen. So, how does this end then? I mean, if Trump doesn't pull back, again, it's hard to say. he made some reference to this but uh again he pretended to do peace negotiations uh uh you know during two surprise attacks. So again, I I I do think at some level he he doesn't want this war that his intentions from not just the first presidency, but the second was to avoid these kind of things, but now that it's in this war and he can't really uh well afford to pull out, it seems. Uh what are the options for Washington here? Can they I mean do they put boots on the ground? Do they do they try to invade Yemen? because you know if Yemen shuts down the Red Sea I know it's a huge country it's like what 34 million people it's I I wouldn't want to enter this country but uh again desperate situation results in foolish policy so what do you expect to happen from here on well one of two things either he announces I've decided unilaterally to end our involvement in this war and I welcome the opportunity to negotiate with the Iranians and to assist in any way possible to return the Persian Gulf to normaly. Uh that's an admission that he made a mistake which is fine. He made a mistake. Uh I don't know if that'll happen or not. Uh personally I would welcome that development but I think the Israel connection really makes it impossible at this stage. Uh we don't understand the connection that Israel has to Trump. I don't understand all of it, but I understand enough to know that yes, you're right. When I spoke to him in uh April of 2020 in the Oval Office, it was supposed to be short turned into a longer session. And I was enormously impressed with the things that he had to say. And one of the things that was very clear to me is he didn't want a war with Iran. In fact, he was looking for some sort of negotiated agreement. And I'm I'm sure he felt that way when he was elected. However, uh these Zionist billionaires were very instrumental in his winning that election. Not only because of the money he was given, his campaign was given. Not only because of the many ways in which his family and he have been enriched by the Zionists, it's also by what they didn't do. They didn't provide any support whatsoever to his opponent. And the last time around when he lost the election or lost bid for re-election, they invested heavily in his opponent. So he knew they could deliver the goods. In other words, if he had them on his side, he could win. But when he did that, he had to make some promises. And I think one of those was that he would back Israel's attack on Iran. Uh whether or not he was aware that Israel's plan is to turn most of the region into the faximile of Gaza, I don't know. But that's really Israel's answer for Iran, Gaza. And wherewise we started out with this decapitation nonsense. And I think a lot of that goes back to Israel. We can get the leadership. If we get the leadership, this will internal encourage internal upheaval and so forth. So then they lied of about 30,000 people being killed. That wasn't true. It was about 3,000. and they had a very important role in stirring up that violence, passing out weapons and encouraging people to shoot at police and so forth. It failed. So then the decision was we'll try the decapitation. That'll probably work. It didn't work. So what is the goal now? Well, since lawyers of London shut down the straight of Hormuz and the Iranians can now charge people to move through it, uh the idea is that you have to destroy Iran. period. The destruction of Iran, the disintegration of its society. And after all, that's what we wanted to do to Russia. That's what the Europeans don't understand. That's what we wanted to do. And when I say we, I'm not talking about the average American. I'm talking about the same people that are backing Trump. Now, we're very interested in the same thing in Russia. Destroy Russia, rape it, divide it, fragment it, steal its resources. Well, now we're turning to Iran with the same purpose in mind. And I just don't know that Donald Trump can get off this train to hell. And that's what he's doing. He's riding a train to hell. But just as a last question, how do you see Russia responding to this? because uh I always make the point the Russians were genuinely very optimistic when Trump got reelected because uh with the Russia gate thing being over and Trump seemingly having the genuine objective of improving bilateral relations with Russia be it either for you know common views on the world in terms of you know conservatism or just pragmatic great power politics that is getting Russia on our side of the ledger instead are pushing it towards Iran. It's nonetheless uh whatever the reason was it seemed genuine and now of course one year into this it's you know the US much like the Europeans are still participating in the proxy war and the surprise attack on Iran appears to have shook the Russians a bit that is the willingness to throw out the rule book kill leaders you know attack nuclear power plants uh across the board they they also lost some faith in the whole diplomacy how do you see Russia responding to this because on one hand I see the disappointment but there's many who also seem to have a hope that uh you know there won't be another Trump someone who really actually wants peace with Russia if he does want peace of course as you said it's a even though it's Trump behind the wheel nonetheless it's the same objective nonetheless it seems to destroy to destroy Russia break it up and seize its resources Well, first of all, the Russians do not understand how the American government works. Very few people beyond the borders of the United States do. The most astute observation that Putin has made is that he's spoken with several presidents and then discovered that nothing really changes because others are in control. Now, some people call it the deep state. Uh, okay. I call it Zionist billionaires, people that are among the wealthiest in the world. They dominate Wall Street. They are financial capitalists. These are not people who made money from building anything, from creating anything. These are the kings of uh financial transaction. And they have enormous quantities of money and they're willing to employ that money in pursuit of their agenda. We talked about what the agenda in Russia originally was. Now we're looking at the same agenda. The good news for them is that if this works, then Israel will be in charge of the entire region in their minds. And that means that they from their positions in New York and London and the banks, they're controlling everything because ultimately that's what they want to do. They want to control the region's resources. Now, my view is that's not going to happen. And I think that the Russians are now figuring out it probably won't happen. If anything, we have demonstrated not our brilliance and our supremacy in every field. We've demonstrated that our superiority isn't that great. That our effectiveness is not what they thought it was. That there are more chinks in our armor than either the Russians or the Chinese or anybody else really really thought. That's the danger of using military power when you don't need to. There was no pressing reason for us to attack Iran. None. And when you have to go back to Netanyahu talking points into 1982 and you know the bombing of a building that contained Marines where the Marine Guards had not even been issued ammunition. What a disaster. That's your justification for destroying a nation of 93 million people. Or that they simply say things you don't like, but they're a threat to Israel. Okay. I think the biggest threat to Israel other than Israel itself at this stage is probably Turkey. And in fact that's what Naftali Bennett has said and even Netanyahu has admitted that which means I guess the expectation is as long as they control us and our armed forces that if we're successful at utterly destroying Iran then we can turn our attention to do the same thing to Turkey. Disaster. Insane. So NATO is finished. NATO died on the battlefields of Ukraine. It's over. The Russians need to wake up and and discover that they not anyone else, but they are going to have to settle this war. The only way I think they can do that is by advancing. So, we'll see in two weeks when the ground is thoroughly dried out whether or not President Putin is willing to finally end the war. And he can do that. He has the military power to do it. There's nothing really standing in his way. He can obliterate all the opposition, but he's got to do it. No one is going to help him. No one is going to offer him a good deal. Doesn't exist. And Trump, no matter what he privately thinks, has demonstrated he can't deliver. Now, we're dealing with the Gulf, and the Russians are watching this. And I think the Russians have always advised the Iranians to be conservative, to cooperate, to negotiate. Whether or not they continue to do that at this stage, I don't know. But I think that now more than ever, the Russians and the Chinese will back Iran. So I see no evidence that whatever we do is going to result in the outcome that the Israelis want. Well, uh, sorry, let me squeeze in one very brief last question. Wait a minute now. You said that was the last, Glenn. This better be a short one. I got to go. Well, uh, I know you hate this word when, uh, well, what is the strategy here of the Europeans, you think? because uh well they they seem to have bet everything on backing the US hoping to win over the love of the Americans but but now suddenly they have this you know they they Iran is essentially the conflict where they decide to turn the back on the US well what do you think is the strategy now because they don't have energy from Russia from the the Middle East their American backup is kind of gone Ukraine is falling apart the Middle East again the mess how How do they make sense of their current positions? Keep in mind that in 1964-65, Prime Minister Harold McMillan was approached and asked whether or not the British would support the the Americans if they intervened in Vietnam and he said very in a very straightforward manner, "Absolutely not. We have no intention of involving ourselves there." Did that destroy our relationship with Britain? No, of course not. But in those times, people understood the limitations of treaties and alliances and the alliance with us pertained primarily to the Soviet threat. So I think I I think it's you're still going to have uh bilateral relations between us and the Europeans regardless of what happens. They buy a lot from us. We sell them a lot. We buy a lot from them. That's still unchanging. But the energy question is critical and they are not prepared to go on the same path to extinction that the Gulf Emirates are on. The Gulf Emirates are finished. I don't see those being rebuilt at all. We are not coming back. That was very clear from Trump when he said, "Well, we'll just leave and that's it. We've done as much as we can or we'll do whatever we can to obliterate Iran, after which we will leave, too." All right. So the Gulf Emirates have bet on the wrong horse and they have cultivated hostility for their big neighbor. They're paying a terrible price for it. The the Germans, the Poles, the French, the British have done the same stupid thing towards Russia. They have cultivated an enemy in the big neighbor to the east. Huge mistake. Unnecessary. You know that. I know that. Russia has no interest in invading or threatening Western Europe. That means you need new governments. You got to get rid of of mounts for God's sakes. I was so happy to see the AFD announced quite recently, we st we support the complete removal of all US forces from Germany. Absolutely should have happened 30 years ago. We should never have stayed in that country. There's no justification for it whatsoever. And I think others are going to reach that conclusion. De Gaul was right. We should remember that. Orbond has been right. The British need a new government badly and so do the French. And before they get that or when they get it, they may end up fighting an internal rebellion of various types and sorts as David Betts has pointed out. I don't know if you've had David Betts on your program or not. I did. But he is absolutely right. So these are things that have to be dealt with that have to be addressed and can only be addressed by the Europeans. said, "While they do this, put an end to the hostility with Russia and tell these maniacs, I like them, they're wonderful people, Lithuanians, Latvians, Estonians, and the Baltic states, stop. Stop the stupidity. You've got to live with your neighbor and get along with your neighbor. It's not that scary an idea." And by the way, that's what Bismar told them back in 1879. Knock it off. Get along with the Russians. whatever you think you're suffering from, it's not as bad as you think it is. And he was right. It wasn't. It got a lot worse under the Soviets. That's a different era, but that was catastrophic. We We got to get out of this business of treating Russia as this perpetual Stalinist threat. They're not. Well, thank you for accommodating my second last question and as always, uh, thank you for your time. You owe me, Glenn. You owe me. I'll make it up to you someday. Thank you. See you. Bye-bye.
